title: Pashtet
subtitle: Russian chicken liver pâté
excerpt: Chicken livers fried with onion, carrot, and thyme, then blended with butter until silky. Russia's everyday pâté — spread on bread for a quick meal, served as a starter at the festive table, and made in five minutes once the livers are in the pan.
sourceNotes: Pashtet (from the French pâté) is a smooth liver spread widely eaten in Russia and Ukraine, typically made from chicken livers cooked with onion and carrot and blended with butter. It is eaten as a spread on bread or as a starter, and is commonly produced commercially — there are many Soviet-era canned versions. The home-made version is considerably better and takes less than 20 minutes. It is a practical dish in the Russian tradition: simple ingredients, short cooking time, good result.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions. Defrost overnight in the fridge and stir well before serving.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Make in a large batch and portion into small containers for the fridge or freezer.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made the day before — the flavour deepens in the fridge overnight. Keeps for 4 days refrigerated.

content.text: Chicken livers need to be cooked through but not overcooked. Pink in the centre is fine and keeps the pâté creamy; grey throughout means they have gone past the point and the texture will be grainy after blending. Two to three minutes per side in a hot pan is enough — they should be firm on the outside and just pink in the very centre.

The butter goes in cold, in pieces, after the livers are blended. Adding it while the mixture is still hot will melt it too fast and the pâté can split. Blend everything together at room temperature and chill to set.

Warm the {{sunflower-oil}} in a frying pan over medium-high heat. Add the {{onion}}, {{carrot}}, {{garlic}}, {{thyme}}, and {{bay-leaves}} and cook for 6 minutes until soft. Increase the heat to high. Add the {{chicken-livers}} in a single layer and cook for 2 to 3 minutes per side until browned outside and just pink in the centre. Remove the bay leaf. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper-ground}}. Leave to cool for 10 minutes.

Transfer the liver mixture to a food processor. Add the cold {{unsalted-butter}} in pieces and blend until very smooth, scraping down the sides. Taste and adjust seasoning. Spoon into a jar or small dish, smooth the surface, and refrigerate for at least 1 hour to firm up. Serve spread on bread or toast.

items.cause: Livers overcooked, or not blended long enough
items.fix: Blend for at least 2 full minutes, scraping down regularly. Slightly undercooked livers produce a smoother result — err on the side of pink rather than grey

items.cause: Too much butter relative to liver, or chilled too long in a cold fridge
items.fix: Remove from the fridge 20 minutes before serving. A pâté that is too hard can be briefly warmed and re-blended with a teaspoon of warm water

items.cause: Livers not trimmed properly, or blood not rinsed off
items.fix: Rinse the livers under cold water, then pat dry before cooking. Remove any green-tinged pieces (bile duct remnants) which are bitter

content.text: Pashtet is practical food: fast to make, keeps in the fridge, good on bread. In the Soviet era it was eaten as a sandwich filling for packed lunches and as a quick weekday starter. It appears on Russian festive tables as part of the cold cuts and spreads that open a celebration meal, alongside smoked salmon, herring under a fur coat, and Olivier salad. The commercial canned version is still widely sold in Russia and Eastern Europe under the same name, but the home-made version bears no resemblance to it.
